checks.a_analysis: The output JSON faithfully represents the text and information found in the source document. There are no invented paragraphs, sentences, or section headings. The metadata title is AI-generated, which is an acceptable change per the instructions.
There is no contact information (phone numbers, email addresses, mailing addresses, or URLs) present in the source document, and none has been added to the output.

All three pages of the source document are represented in the output. Section 1 corresponds to Page 1, Section 2 to Page 2, and Section 3 to Page 3. All major text blocks, legends, and images are included.

The tables in the output correspond to the legend and key information provided in the source. Table 1 (Phase & Quality legend) contains all 5 items from the source. Table 2 (Quality Level definition) contains the single key-value pair. Table 3 (2025 Legend) contains all 4 legend entries from the Page 2 map. No rows are missing.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: All critical text elements from the sections are present. The detailed list of project status updates on Page 3 is fully captured in the list element of Section 3. The notes on Page 1 regarding datums are captured as paragraphs. Text within graphics (like the 'Data available February 2025' box on the Page 1 map) is appropriately handled through image alt text.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the JSON matches the source document's wording. No changes were found that alter the meaning of the information provided.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output JSON against the source PDF for each page. The headings, legends (rendered as tables), paragraphs, and list items appear in the output exactly as they do in the source without any unintended duplication. While the heading '2025 Data Collection Area' appears twice in the JSON, it correctly reflects its appearance on both Page 2 and Page 3 of the source document. No text blocks are repeated within the output that are not repeated in the source.

The output uses H1 tags for the main title on Page 1 and for the slide titles on Pages 2 and 3. There are no skips in the hierarchy (e.g., H1 to H3). This flat hierarchy is consistent with a presentation-style document where each page/slide has a primary title. The hierarchy is logical and supports accessibility.

The document contains two legends which have been correctly converted into table structures. On Page 1, the table 'Phase & Quality' correctly identifies the header and the five data rows. On Page 2, the legend is converted into a single-column table. Both structures accurately represent the logical relationship between the legend keys and their headers. No columns or rows are missing or misaligned.

The reading order follows a logical top-to-bottom, left-to-right flow for each page. On Page 1, it sequences the title, legend, main text paragraphs, and then the map image. This linearization is appropriate for the layout and ensures the information is presented in a coherent manner.

The bulleted list on Page 3 is correctly identified as an unordered list in the JSON. It contains all 8 items present in the source document, and the content of each list item matches the source text accurately.
The source document does not contain any hyperlinks. Accordingly, no links were expected or found in the output JSON. No fabricated links were injected.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output converts the multi-element layout of the PDF pages (floating text boxes, legends integrated with maps, logos in corners) into a single-column flow. This is a standard and acceptable practice for accessible HTML. The organization of information remains logical and matches the source's intent, with content from each page grouped together in the output.

The map legends from the PDF are rendered as HTML tables. While the text content is present and readable, the critical visual color indicators (color swatches) that link the text to the map regions are missing in the HTML tables (e.g., 'Phase & Quality' and 'Western NC Contractor Tile Extents'). This rendering omission makes the tables ineffective as map legends for a sighted user.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 1 and Page 2 contain map legends with color swatches (e.g., orange for Phase 1, light blue for Phase 2, etc.)
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML tables 'Table: Phase & Quality' and 'Table: Western NC Contractor Tile Extents' contain only text and lack any color indicators or swatches.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections 1 and 2, tables for map legends

checks.a_analysis: Several meaningful visual elements from the source PDF are missing in the HTML rendering. Specifically, on Page 1, two floating text boxes ('QL 1 data collection completed' and 'Data available February 2025') that appear over the map are absent. Additionally, the north arrow and scale bar for the primary map on Page 1 are missing, although they are present in the second map rendered from Page 2.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 1 map includes floating text boxes: 'QL 1 data collection completed' and 'Data available February 2025', plus a north arrow and scale bar.
checks.b_evidence_output: The first map image and surrounding text in the HTML 'NC Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR)...' section completely omit these text boxes, the north arrow, and the scale bar.
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 1, 'NC Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) Elevation Data 2024 Data Collection'

checks.a_analysis: There is no unintended content duplication. While the map 'High Resolution Topographic Refresh Western North Carolina' appears twice in the HTML, this is a faithful reproduction of the source PDF where the same map is featured on both Page 2 and Page 3. Heading repetitions match the page titles in the source.
